ash each other's foreheads.<|19.98|> <|20.70|> I'm not going to lie, it made me feel pretty good about my crosses,<|23.76|> <|24.26|> and at least by that measure, I have some job security.<|27.04|> beautiful place.<|14.62|> <|14.62|> It never ceases to amaze me and fill me with awe and just this sense of good fortune that<|22.24|> <|22.24|> this is where I get to be week in and week eye.<|12.22|> <|13.12|> And something was sparkling right at the top of the arch on the east side of the crossing, right below the Clara Story window.<|22.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> And it took a while to figure out what it might be.<|3.74|> <|4.52|> But at last, I knew.<|6.80|> <|8.70|> There, on that first day of Lent,<|12.92|> <|13.56|> that somber time of admitting<|15.98|> <|15.98|> and coming to terms with the reality of our mortality,<|20.80|> <|21.64|> ashes to ashes, dust to dust,<|24.80|> <|25.50|> I was seeing a sign of Christ.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Christmas.<|2.28|> <|2.28|> Because perched atop that arch at the east side of the crossing, glinting in the light,<|8.80|> <|8.80|> was a glittered wooden star from our children's Christmas pageant.<|15.48|> <|15.48|> The very stars that are scattered here around the creche.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> And it's still there.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No idea how it got there. No idea how long it's been there.<|4.90|> <|5.82|> We've had lifts and cranes in here for wall cleanings and stained glass window maintenance and lighting projects and more over the last many years.<|16.76|> <|17.94|> Who thought to perch that little star there?<|23.80|> <|23.80|> No.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Where did they find it?<|1.80|> <|2.68|> Was it meant to be whimsical or theological?<|7.22|> <|8.22|> A joke or an acknowledgement that there is always a bit of Christmas happening every time we gather?<|16.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> even on Ash Wednesday.<|3.22|> <|4.94|> I find this paradox, this finding a Christmas star on Ash Wednesday,<|10.76|> <|11.72|> especially meaningful this morning<|14.72|> <|14.72|> as we encounter this gospel lesson from Matthew.<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm not going to lie again, I sometimes wonder if I've done something wrong, because I've<|6.68|> <|6.68|> now gotten this text twice in about as many years.<|11.08|> <|11.08|> And this text is telling a horrible story in the midst of Christmas.<|19.68|> <|19.68|> The story of the Holy Family's terror and the slaughter of the innocents.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the shepherds have returned to their flocks in the genocide and exile comes on this, the ninth day of Christmas during<|12.80|> <|12.80|> a season of hope and Emmanuel, a Christmas star on what feels for all the world like<|23.86|> <|23.86|> an Ash Wednesday.<|24.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.54|> I do think this story in Matthew keeps the Christmas story real.<|7.14|> <|7.74|> It keeps it from becoming just a story that is because Joseph is wise<|23.60|> <|23.60|> and he knows that Herod's son is as evil and power-hungry as<|29.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> as his father. It's another close call, another disaster averted, but of course,<|8.74|> <|9.34|> not without tremendous fear and anxiety and no small amount of looking over their shoulder,<|18.90|> <|18.90|> making sure they're not followed.<|20.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.58|> I can't help but think at this moment of our neighbors to the Northwest<|8.52|> <|8.52|> who fled their homes on Thursday evening, looking for shelter and refuge,<|16.08|> <|17.42|> evacuating on roads that are still bedecked with Christmas decorations,<|22.12|> <|23.00|> passing homes with colored lights and inflatable Santas,<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> as they were fleeing, driving away from fire and smoke,<|5.66|> <|6.56|> not sure when they would be able to return,<|9.06|> <|10.24|> not sure to what they would be returning.<|12.98|> <|14.98|> I think about that star perched up on that arch,<|19.20|> <|19.20|> and<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A little bit of Christmas in the midst of all that happens here with us as a community.<|8.10|> <|9.02|> Regular Sunday worship, baptisms, soaring anthems, and Easter joy.<|16.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> a little bit of Christmas in the midst of all that happens here for us as a community,<|7.34|> <|8.18|> Ash Wednesdays, Lent, Good Fridays.<|12.38|> <|14.22|> That little bit of Christmas will also be here<|19.56|> <|19.56|> when many of us gather to finally mourn the loss<|24.32|> <|24.32|> of the wonderful soul, Canon Burt Womack,<|28.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> this coming Saturday. A little bit of Christmas that glimmers and sparkles in the midst of sorrow<|8.44|> <|8.44|> and mourning and suffering, much like the gospel reading today.<|14.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Bye.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I was curious about the Clara story window under which it rests.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> And thanks to Ann Jones' fabulous book about all of our stained glass windows,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> I didn't have to look far for my answer.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> That stained glass window is called the New Testament children's window.<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.76|> And among those depicted in stained glass are Simeon, holding the infant Jesus, flanked by Mary and Joseph.<|9.28|> <|10.50|> John the Baptist is there with his parents.<|13.42|> <|14.80|> Adult Jesus is also depicted with Jairus' daughter after recessions.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> her and with the child offering his precious few loaves and fish for the<|8.58|> <|8.58|> feeding of so many they are stories of prophecy and stories of miracle it's<|16.68|> <|16.68|> lovely and I love that the star rests below this window<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> Perhaps most appropriate to this day, though, in today's difficult text, at the bottom of<|11.40|> <|11.40|> that window, there's a row of nine children, nine little boys who would be no more than<|21.98|> <|21.98|> two years old.<|22.98|> <|24.36|> They're the holy innocents of today's book.<|27.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> gospel reading. Ash Wednesday and a Christmas star. A reminder for us on this day and every day<|13.92|> <|13.92|> that suffering and joy are intimately entwined throughout our entire story of faith. Amen.<|26.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.10|><|endoftext|>
